# Transcrição: 01. Start With WHY

- **Fonte:** 01. Start With WHY
- **Duração:** 03:07
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Welcome back. We hope you took some time to live with your why statement. Perhaps you even shared it with someone. If you did, how did it feel? Did it feel like you? Did it roll off your tongue? Did it feel awkward? Some or all of these things are normal in the beginning. The important thing is that you took some time to let it sink in. Our natural tendency is to start with the tangible. We define our value by what we do. So it takes practice to start with why. Like riding a bike. At first we're unsure and unsteady. We're concerned with all the things we need to do. Pedal fast enough. Maintain our balance and keep from crashing. In the beginning we have to really concentrate. We may fall over and we may even scrape our knees. But we get back on the bike and we try again. And eventually it becomes natural and we just ride. Starting with why is exactly the same. At first it feels awkward. We may even wonder if it's actually working. But with practice it becomes the foundation for how we present ourselves. Over time it will become so natural you won't be able to remember when you couldn't do it. Just like riding a bike. I remember the first few times I shared my why. While I was excited to share it, it sometimes confused people and they would look at me as if I were a bit weird. Through trying an error it's getting better. I'm getting better at answering the question, what do you do? I most often ask that question when I'm on a plane. It happened recently on a flight back home from Boston. I was chatting to a few of the cabin crew in the galley somewhere in mid-Atlantic. One of them asked me what I did. I could have just told him my job, what I did. Instead I led with my why. I enable people to be extraordinary so that they can do extraordinary things. I then followed this with one of the ways I do this is to teach leaders and organizations how to create cultures where everyone has an opportunity to make their greatest contribution. And here is one I've learned. People who believe what I believe only hear one word, extraordinary. And when they do, we end up talking about our shared beliefs for the next two hours and they end up asking for the website address where they can learn more. It certainly helps the flights to pass quickly. As you become more and more comfortable with your why, you'll be able to get creative in how you express it while staying true to what it is you believe. So here's what you're going to do next. Enter the current version of your why statement. If it's the exact same as your final draft in part two, that's great. If you made any tweaks in the language, be sure to make the changes. Maybe you found another word or phrase that feels a little more authentic to you. Perhaps one of your other themes actually ended up feeling more inspiring to you than what you originally put in your first draft. Whatever the tweaks, the goal is to get closer and closer to

## [03:03 - 03:07]

a statement that you love. A statement that feels right.
